entity: Fort Des Moines Provisional Army Officer Training School
summary: I was sent to Fort Des Moines, Iowa, with fifteen black WAACs from Baltimore for basic training and officer candidate school. It was exciting because we had black officers and trained alongside white officers in the same facility and barracks, which was unusual at the time. The training was rigorous and equal to that of men, including learning military courtesy, history, and how to shoot an M1 rifle. This experience marked a significant moment in my military career during the segregated Army of the Second World War.

I was part of the Women's Army Auxiliary Corps, a segregated unit during the Second World War, where black enlisted women were limited to laundry or hospital units with menial tasks. As an officer, I fought for promotions and better treatment for the black WAACs, even working alongside them to highlight their hardships. The WAAC experience was both challenging and empowering, as we pushed against discrimination and helped rewrite service rules to improve conditions for women. It was a difficult but formative time in my life and military service.

During basic training at Fort Des Moines, we learned how to shoot the M1 Garand rifle, just like the men did. It was part of the rigorous training that proved we could handle the same military tasks as male soldiers. The M1 was a symbol of our equality in training despite the segregation and discrimination we faced. Learning to use it was both a practical skill and a statement of our capability as soldiers.

I took a course in personnel administration at Purdue University during my military service, but each time I arrived, the president of the school would quit. Despite being the only black trainee, I persisted in seeking additional training opportunities. Attending Purdue was part of my effort to advance my skills and career, even though racial barriers were evident. It was a challenging but important step in my professional development.

I was sent to the College of William & Mary in Virginia for training, but like at Purdue, the president quit when I arrived. I was the only black person there, and it was unthinkable for a black person to be present without a menial role. Despite this, I stayed in the same facilities as the white trainees, breaking racial norms of the time. This experience showed me the deep segregation in education and military training during the war.

I was sent to Washington and Lee University to become a special service officer, and once again, I was the only black trainee. The president quit upon my arrival, reflecting the racial tensions and resistance to integration. Despite these challenges, I completed my training and took on the role, which was rare for a black woman at the time. This was part of my ongoing effort to gain skills and serve effectively in the Army during the Second World War.

At Fort Breckinridge, Kentucky, I was assigned as the mess officer for the colored WAC company, despite having no mess training. The post commander, Colonel Throckmorton, initially refused to send me for training and treated me with disrespect, but I stood my ground firmly. I later became assistant to the post publications officer, a nonexistent position, where I organized Army regulations and earned commendations. Fort Breckinridge was a place where I faced and challenged discrimination head-on.

I requested to be sent to Fort Eustis, Virginia, for mess training so I could properly serve as mess officer at Fort Breckinridge, but Colonel Throckmorton refused. Despite this, I proved my capability in that role without formal training. Fort Eustis represented a missed opportunity for me due to racial and institutional barriers, but I persevered regardless. It was part of the broader struggle I faced as a black female officer in the segregated Army.

Many people called me from all over the country saying my name was on the list to go to Europe and that they would see me at Fort McClellan. However, I never received orders to report there because my name was taken off the list due to my conflict with Colonel Throckinmorton. Fort McClellan was a symbol of the opportunities denied to me despite my qualifications and service. It was frustrating to be excluded from overseas deployment during the war.

After Camp Breckinridge closed, I was sent to Fort Knox, where Colonel Throckinmorton was now post commander. Surprisingly, he appointed me as the post special service officer, giving me an office in headquarters, a secretary, a driver, and a station wagon. My role was to liaise with famous civilian entertainers like Duke Ellington and Lena Horne to bring them to entertain the troops. Fort Knox was where I found a unique position to improve morale and break down racial barriers through entertainment.

As the special service officer at Fort Knox, I worked to bring legendary entertainers like Duke Ellington to perform for the troops. I met their planes, arranged accommodations, and ensured they had a place to stay since they couldn't stay on post or in white hotels. Duke Ellington's performances were vital for troop morale, providing a welcome break from the routine military life. It was an honor to facilitate such important cultural moments during the war.

Stan Kenton was one of the big-name entertainers I helped bring to Fort Knox to perform for the soldiers. Like the others, he couldn't stay on post or in white hotels, so I arranged for him to stay in local colored hotels or private homes. His shows were a crucial morale booster during the war, giving troops a chance to enjoy music and forget the hardships of military life. Working with Stan Kenton was part of my role in supporting the soldiers' well-being.

Lena Horne was among the famous entertainers I coordinated to come to Fort Knox to perform for the troops. Because of segregation, she stayed in colored hotels or private homes rather than on the post or in white hotels. Her performances were uplifting and helped improve morale during the difficult times of the Second World War. I took pride in ensuring she and other entertainers were well cared for despite the racial barriers of the era.

Earl Hines was one of the legendary musicians I helped bring to Fort Knox to entertain the troops. Like the others, he faced segregation and had to stay in colored accommodations off-post. His music was a vital part of lifting spirits during the war, and I was responsible for coordinating his visit and ensuring his needs were met. This role allowed me to contribute to the war effort in a unique and meaningful way.

Jimmy Lunceford was another famous entertainer I arranged to perform at Fort Knox for the troops. Due to segregation, he stayed in colored hotels or private homes rather than on base or in white hotels. His performances were important for morale, providing a joyful escape for soldiers during the war. Coordinating with artists like Lunceford was a key part of my duties as special service officer.

Cab Calloway was one of the legendary entertainers I helped bring to Fort Knox to perform for the soldiers. Like the others, he was subject to segregation and had to stay in colored accommodations off-post. His music was a highlight for the troops, boosting morale during the difficult war years. I took care to ensure he was welcomed and accommodated despite the racial challenges of the time.

Count Basie was among the great musicians I coordinated to entertain troops at Fort Knox. Due to segregation, he stayed off-post in colored hotels or private homes. His performances were a vital morale boost for soldiers during the war, offering a respite from military life. Helping to bring Count Basie and other legends to the post was one of the most rewarding parts of my service.

It took an act of the United States Congress to get me promoted beyond second lieutenant, which shows how difficult it was for black women to advance in the Army during the war. Congress also passed the legislation that commissioned me as an officer and gave me the same status as white officers. The role of Congress was crucial in shaping my military career, even though many barriers remained. Their actions were a step toward equality, but the struggle continued on the ground.

My entire military experience took place during the Second World War, a time when the Army was segregated and black women faced tremendous discrimination. Despite these challenges, I trained at Fort Des Moines, served at various posts like Fort Breckinridge and Fort Knox, and worked to improve conditions for black WAACs. The war was a backdrop for my growth, struggles, and achievements as a black female officer. It was a time of both hardship and progress in the fight for equality.
